era_context: The Old Kingdom of Egypt under the Sixth Dynasty is in its final phase. The Akkadian Empire in Mesopotamia is experiencing significant internal strain and external pressure from Gutian invasions. The Indus Valley Civilization is at its mature phase. This period falls within the late third millennium BCE, characterized by major political transitions across multiple civilizations.

events.title: Sixth Dynasty Egypt: provincial autonomy increases; central authority weakens
events.description: The Sixth Dynasty of Egypt continues to experience erosion of central authority as provincial governors consolidate power. Inscriptions from provincial tombs increasingly assert local autonomy. The economic resources of the state are stretched by continued monument-building and military campaigns. This period represents the ongoing transition from the centralized Old Kingdom to the decentralized First Intermediate Period.
key_figures: Pepi II (Pharaoh, if traditional chronology is accepted)
events.certainty_note: The dating of the Sixth Dynasty's decline is approximate. The archaeological evidence for provincial autonomy is clear, but the precise year is uncertain.
cross_references: Part of broader Sixth Dynasty decline

events.title: Akkadian Empire: internal collapse and Gutian invasions intensify
events.description: The Akkadian Empire experiences accelerating internal collapse as Gutian raiders intensify their incursions into Mesopotamia. City-states assert increasing independence. The central authority of the Akkadian kings weakens further. Archaeological evidence of destruction layers at multiple sites suggests widespread conflict and instability.
events.certainty_note: The Akkadian collapse is attested archaeologically and in later texts, but precise dating within this period is uncertain.

events.title: Indus Valley Civilization: mature urban phase; extensive trade networks active
events.description: The Indus Valley Civilization remains in its mature phase with large urban centres, standardized administrative systems, and extensive trade networks. Archaeological evidence indicates continued prosperity and stability.
events.certainty_note: The archaeological evidence for the Indus Valley Civilization's mature phase is well-established.
cross_references: Part of broader Indus Valley Civilization period

disconfirming_evidence: The traditional chronology of the Sixth Dynasty is contested. Some scholars argue for a much shorter reign for Pepi II, which would place this year after his death. The Akkadian collapse is sometimes portrayed as a sudden catastrophe, but archaeological evidence suggests a more gradual process of fragmentation and infiltration.
historiographic_note: The historical record for -2149 is sparse. Egyptian chronology remains debated. Mesopotamian sources are limited to inscriptions and archaeological evidence. The Indus Valley Civilization is known only through archaeology. All three regions lack detailed contemporary narratives of specific events in this year.

graph_edges.note: The fragmentation of the Sixth Dynasty continued from -2149 into -2148.
The Akkadian collapse continued from -2149 into -2148.
